OrderItem.title: OrderItem
OrderItem.description: Represents an individual item ordered at Mirinha's Restaurant.

id.description: Unique identifier for the order item.

itemName.description: The name of the item ordered.

itemGroup.description: The group the item belongs to (Fiados salão, Fiados rua, Vendas rua, Vendas salão).

quantity.description: The quantity of the item ordered. Defaults to 1 if not specified.

orderDateTime.description: The date and time the item was ordered.

definition.description: Stores order items specific to a user.  Allows each user to only access their own order items. Path based ownership: `/users/{userId}/order_items/{orderItemId}` enables secure `list` operations based on `request.auth.uid == userId`.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique identifier of the user.

params.name: orderItemId
params.description: The unique identifier of the order item.

firestore.reasoning:
The Firestore structure is designed to address the 'Missing or insufficient permissions' error by ensuring clear ownership and appropriate data segregation.  The core issue is the 'list' operation on `/order_items` failing, indicating a lack of read access.  Given the provided app requirements, all `order_items` should be associated with a user. To solve the authorization problem and support listing, I'm creating a user-centric data structure where order items are stored under each user's document. This approach inherently limits access to a user's own data, preventing unauthorized listing.

**Authorization Independence:** The structure ensures authorization independence because access control is based on the path (`/users/{userId}/order_items/{orderItemId}`) and the authenticated user's ID. There is no reliance on data from other documents to determine authorization.

**QAPs (Rules are not Filters):** To secure the `list` operation, the structure places order items under a user-specific collection (`/users/{userId}/order_items`). This allows a security rule that grants `list` access only if `request.auth.uid == userId`, effectively ensuring that users can only list their own order items.

This structure inherently creates the segregation required for secure `list` operations, ensuring rules are not used as filters.
